content.full_text:
May 6, 1977
A-77-05-028

Mr. Milton Jacobs
Koblic & Jacobs
Fox Plaza, Suite 703
San Francisco, California 94102

Dear Mr. Jacobs:

This letter is in response to your request for information
concerning political contributions and gifts made by corpora-
tions to candidates for public office.

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit corporations from
making gifts or contributions to the campaigns of candidates
for state or local public office. There may, however, be
certain reporting requirements incurred as a result of making
these contributions. If a corporation receives funds from
stockholders, officers or any other source for the purpose of
contributing to candidates for public office, it would incur
reporting obligations under Section 82013(a) as a resident
committee. If the corporation made in-kind or non-monetary
contribution(s) of $500 or more in any calendar year to candi-
date(s) for public office the corporation would qualify as an
expenditure committee and incur filing obligations under
82013(b) of the Political Reform Act.

If the corporation made a direct monetary contribution(s) to
a candidate(s) for public office in the amount of $5,000 in
any calendar year, it would incur filing obligations under
Section 82013(c) of the Political Reform Act.

Corporations qualifying as committees under Section 82013(b)
or 82013(c) are committees for a calendar year only. Corpora-
tions which collect funds for future contributions to candidates
for public office and, therefore, incur filing obligations
under 82013(a) are committees until they terminate.

Any monetary of non-monetary contribution by other than a
82013(a) filer under the two specified thresholds ($500 under
82013(b) or $5,000 under 82013(c)) would not result in a
filing obligation by the contributing corporation.

/2

Corporations which employ a lobbyist registered with the
Secretary of State incur certain reporting obligations when
making gifts to state officials. If a corporation does not
employ a lobbyist and wishes to make a gift to a state
official there is no prohibition or reporting requirement.
The state official, however, is obligated to list all gifts
received from any source with a value of $25 or more.

I have enclosed a copy of the Political Reform Act as
amended and other information which should be of an assis-
tance to you. If you require additional information please
do not hesitate to let me know.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator

RSK:bw

Enclosure

KOBLICK & JACOBS
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

MILTON JACOBS
RICHARD D. SPOTSWOOD
LARRY J. SHAPIRO

SUITE 703-FOX PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94102
PHONE (415) 552-1375

April 25, 1977


Fair Political Practices Committee
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

We are seeking information regarding political contributions
and gifts made to candidates for public office by corporations.
It is our understanding that Proposition 9 reflects some of
the law of the State of California in this regard, and we
would ask that if the above is correct, that you forward a
copy of Proposition 9 to this office.

Additionally, if you have any other relevant information con-
cerning corporate gifts and contributions for political pur-
poses or to political candidates, we would appreciate your
sending to this office such other appropriate information.

Thanking you for your courtesy and attention to this request.
We are,

Very truly yours,

KOBLICK & JACOBS

By Milton Jacobs

MJ:sp

If a corporation receives funds from stockholders, officers or any other source for the purpose of contributing to candidates for public office, it would incur reporting obligations under Section 82013(a) as a recipient committee.
